food_name: Ca Kho To (Vietnamese Caramelised Catfish in Clay Pot)

local_names.malay: Ikan Masak Karamel Claypot Vietnam (Ca Kho To)

aliases: Ikan Masak Karamel Claypot Vietnam (Ca Kho To)

malay: Ikan Masak Karamel Claypot Vietnam (Ca Kho To)

food_subgroup: Braised caramelised catfish (pangasius / basa) in clay pot with fish sauce and palm sugar caramel sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Ca Kho To (Vietnamese caramelised clay pot fish). FOODON_03315456 (braised fish dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Braised fish — closest parent; no FoodEx2 code specific to Vietnamese caramelised clay pot fish exists
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0AAV is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for braised fish preparations. No specific code for Ca Kho To exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Ca Kho To or Vietnamese braised fish preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ca Kho To is approximately 150g of braised catfish with caramel-fish sauce, served alongside steamed white rice (rice counted separately).

nutrient_source.title: Ca Kho To — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation (pangasius catfish, fish sauce, palm sugar)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 for pangasius catfish and Southeast Asian fish sauce, supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for palm sugar and shallots. Component calculation applied to reflect the braised dish composition at ~150g serving including sauce reduction. Both FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of palm sugar and mixed fish-sauce braised fish dish — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar (GI ~35) and protein-fat dominant braised fish preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ca Kho To in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~35 is estimated from: (1) palm sugar GI ~35–40 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for coconut/palm sugar; (2) the dominant macronutrient profile of the dish is protein (16g/100g) and fat (6.7g/100g), which substantially blunt glycaemic response; (3) carbohydrate content is 9.3g/100g, predominantly from palm sugar caramel. GL per 150g serving = (35 × 14g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.9 (VERY LOW). This analogous estimate is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium density — ~700mg sodium per 100g; ~1,050mg per 150g serving (52.5% of the 2,000mg daily limit from one dish alone)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should treat Ca Kho To as a high-sodium dish and limit portion size to 75–100g, pair with unsalted steamed rice and low-sodium vegetables, and avoid adding additional fish sauce or soy sauce at the table; the fish sauce reduction process concentrates sodium dramatically and a single standard serving can consume more than half the recommended daily sodium allowance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~35) and very low glycaemic load per serving (~4.9) due to palm sugar caramel and dominant protein-fat macronutrient profile
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ca Kho To itself has an excellent glycaemic profile (GI ~35, GL ~4.9 per 150g serving) and is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled; the primary glycaemic concern is the co-consumed white rice (GL ~32 per 200g), and patients should count the rice separately and consider reducing rice portion or substituting with lower-GI alternatives such as brown rice or cauliflower rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1,050mg per serving) is the dominant cardiovascular risk factor; saturated fat is modest (1.5g/100g); catfish provides lean protein with low omega-3 EPA+DHA content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~1,050mg per 150g serving) is the primary concern as it contributes to elevated blood pressure and fluid retention; the saturated fat content is low (1.5g/100g) and the lean catfish protein is cardioprotective, but the dish should be consumed in reduced portions (75–100g) and paired with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load; catfish provides modest cardiovascular benefit compared to high-omega-3 marine fish such as salmon or mackerel.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (~1,050mg per serving) is a concern for patients on corticosteroids; modest anti-inflammatory benefit from fish protein, vitamin D (~7.5mcg per serving), and selenium (~30mcg per serving)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroid therapy should be cautious of the high sodium content (~1,050mg per serving) which exacerbates corticosteroid-induced fluid retention and hypertension; the vitamin D content (~7.5mcg per 150g serving) and selenium (~30mcg) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit, but catfish is low in omega-3 EPA+DHA (<0.2g/100g) compared to marine fatty fish, limiting its anti-inflammatory potency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ca Kho To (Cá Kho Tộ) is a quintessential Southern Vietnamese braised fish dish cooked in an unglazed clay pot. Catfish (cá tra / pangasius) or basa is slowly braised in a dark, intensely flavoured sauce built from palm sugar caramelised to a deep amber, then shocked with fish sauce (nước mắm) to create a savoury-sweet-umami lacquer. Shallots, garlic, chilli, and black pepper are added to the braise. The dish is cooked over low heat until the sauce reduces to a thick, glossy glaze coating the fish. The result is deeply caramelised, tender fish with a sauce that is simultaneously sweet, salty, and umami-rich. Per 150g serving (fish + sauce, WITHOUT rice): approximately 305 kcal, 24g protein, 10g fat, 14g carbohydrate, GI ~35 (LOW), GL ~4.9 (VERY LOW), ~1,050mg sodium. The glycaemic profile of the dish itself is excellent — the concern is always the co-consumed white rice. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: sodium density is extreme (~700mg/100g) due to fish sauce reduction; a single serving delivers 52.5% of the 2,000mg daily sodium limit. Palm sugar (not cane sugar) lowers the GI slightly. Catfish is a lean freshwater fish with modest omega-3 content. Always served alongside plain steamed white rice — rice must be counted separately. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy via component calculation for the braised dish. No single approved-source entry exists for Ca Kho To as a complete prepared dish; values represent a defensible component-based estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database palm sugar entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry exists in a single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ca Kho To as a prepared braised dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the dish level; individual pangasius catfish fatty acid data exist but sauce-reduction effects on fatty acid composition are not published. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the finished braised dish with reduced sauce is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation — catfish and palm sugar caramel contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central pangasius entries. All core macros (protein_g 16.0g, fat_g 6.7g, carbohydrate_g 9.3g) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a braised catfish dish with caramel-fish sauce. Sodium is extremely high (~700mg/100g; ~1,050mg per 150g serving) due to fish sauce reduction concentration — this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. GI ~35 is an analogous estimate based on palm sugar GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Ca Kho To exists in the published literature. Two portions are defined: 150g (standard serving with sauce) and 180g (small claypot side dish). Only one portion is marked is_default in the output serving section (150g). Vietnamese cuisine requires Vietnamese local name — satisfied by 'Cá Kho Tộ'.
